back:
Distributed system can guarantee only 2 of 3:

• Consistency — every read gets latest write
• Availability — every request gets a response
• Partition Tolerance — works despite network splits

P is unavoidable → choose CP or AP:
  CP: bank systems (strong consistency)
  AP: social media feeds (eventual consistency)

Vertical: bigger machine (more CPU/RAM)
  + Simple, no code changes
  − Hardware limits, single point of failure

Horizontal: more machines
  + Unlimited scale, fault tolerant
  − Complexity: load balancing, data consistency

Rule: scale vertically first, horizontally when needed.
Stateless services → easy horizontal scaling.

front: Latency Numbers Every Dev Should Know
back:
L1 cache ref:        ~1 ns
L2 cache ref:        ~4 ns
RAM ref:             ~100 ns
SSD random read:     ~16 μs
HDD seek:            ~2 ms
Same datacenter RTT: ~0.5 ms
Cross-continent RTT: ~150 ms

Key takeaway:
  Memory > SSD > Network > Disk
  Cache aggressively, minimize network hops.

Distribute data across nodes on a hash ring.

1. Hash nodes & keys onto ring (0 to 2³²)
2. Key maps to next clockwise node
3. Add/remove node → only K/N keys remap

Virtual nodes: each physical node gets multiple
points on ring → better distribution.

Used by: DynamoDB, Cassandra, CDNs

Algorithms:
• Round Robin — simple rotation
• Weighted RR — proportional to capacity
• Least Connections — route to least busy
• IP Hash — sticky sessions
• Random — surprisingly effective

Layers:
  L4 (TCP) — fast, no content inspection
  L7 (HTTP) — content-based routing

Tools: Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ALB/NLB

Read patterns:
• Cache-Aside: app checks cache → miss → read DB → populate cache
• Read-Through: cache auto-fetches from DB on miss

Write patterns:
• Write-Through: write cache + DB together (consistent, slower)
• Write-Behind: write cache, async flush to DB (fast, risk loss)
• Write-Around: write DB only, invalidate cache

Eviction: LRU, LFU, TTL
Tools: Redis, Memcached

"Two hard things: cache invalidation & naming things"

Problems:
• Stale data — TTL + event-based invalidation
• Cache stampede — locking, pre-warming
• Hot key — replicate across shards
• Cold start — pre-populate on deploy

Patterns:
• TTL (time-to-live): simple, eventual consistency
• Pub/Sub invalidation: DB change → notify cache
• Versioned keys: user:123:v5

SQL (Relational):
  + ACID, joins, structured schemas
  + Strong consistency, mature tooling
  − Hard to scale horizontally
  Use: transactions, complex queries
  Ex: PostgreSQL, MySQL

NoSQL:
  + Flexible schema, horizontal scale
  + High throughput, low latency
  − Limited joins, eventual consistency
  Types: Document (Mongo), KV (Redis),
    Column (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j)

Split data across multiple DB instances.

Strategies:
• Hash-based: hash(key) % N shards
• Range-based: A-M shard1, N-Z shard2
• Directory-based: lookup table maps key→shard

Challenges:
• Joins across shards → denormalize
• Rebalancing when adding shards
• Hot shards → consistent hashing

Start with read replicas before sharding.

Copies of data across multiple nodes.

Single-Leader:
  Leader handles writes, replicas handle reads
  + Simple | − Leader is bottleneck

Multi-Leader:
  Multiple write nodes, sync between them
  + Multi-region writes | − Conflict resolution

Leaderless:
  Read/write to multiple nodes, quorum
  W + R > N for consistency
  Ex: Dynamo-style (Cassandra)

Message Queue (point-to-point):
  Producer → Queue → Consumer
  Exactly-once delivery, decoupling
  Ex: RabbitMQ, SQS

Event Streaming (pub/sub + log):
  Producer → Topic → Multiple Consumers
  Ordered, replayable, partitioned
  Ex: Kafka, Kinesis

Use queues for: task processing, async jobs
Use streaming for: event sourcing, real-time pipelines

• Topic: named feed of messages
• Partition: ordered, immutable log within topic
• Offset: position of message in partition
• Consumer Group: parallel consumers, each reads unique partitions
• Replication Factor: copies across brokers

Guarantees:
  Order within partition (not across)
  At-least-once delivery (default)

Scale: add partitions = more parallelism

REST:
  HTTP + JSON, resource-based URLs
  + Simple, cacheable, universal
  − Over/under-fetching

gRPC:
  HTTP/2 + Protobuf, service-based
  + Fast, typed, streaming, code-gen
  − Browser support, debugging harder

GraphQL:
  Single endpoint, client specifies shape
  + Flexible queries, no over-fetching
  − Complexity, caching harder

Use REST for public APIs, gRPC for microservices,
GraphQL for complex frontend needs.

• Token Bucket: tokens refill at rate R,
  request takes a token. Allows bursts.

• Leaky Bucket: requests queue, process at
  fixed rate. Smooths traffic.

• Fixed Window: count requests per time window.
  Edge burst problem at window boundary.

• Sliding Window Log: track each request timestamp.
  Accurate but memory-heavy.

• Sliding Window Counter: hybrid of fixed +
  weighted previous window. Best tradeoff.

Communication:
• Sync: REST/gRPC between services
• Async: events via message queue

Resilience:
• Circuit Breaker: stop calling failing service
• Retry with backoff: exponential + jitter
• Bulkhead: isolate failures

Data:
• Database per service (loose coupling)
• Saga pattern for distributed transactions
• CQRS: separate read/write models

Observability: distributed tracing, centralized logs

Single entry point for all client requests.

Responsibilities:
• Routing to correct microservice
• Authentication & authorization
• Rate limiting & throttling
• Request/response transformation
• SSL termination
• Caching & compression

Tools: Kong, AWS API Gateway, Nginx

BFF (Backend for Frontend):
  Separate gateway per client type
  (web, mobile, 3rd party)

Object Storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob):
  Store images, videos, backups
  Flat namespace, HTTP access
  Virtually unlimited, cheap
  99.999999999% durability (S3)

CDN (CloudFront, Cloudflare, Akamai):
  Cache content at edge locations
  Reduce latency for global users
  Static assets + API acceleration

Pattern: upload to S3 → serve via CDN
Signed URLs for private content

Horizontal (Sharding):
  Split rows across nodes
  Each shard has same schema
  Ex: users 1-1M shard1, 1M-2M shard2

Vertical:
  Split columns/tables by feature
  Ex: user_profile vs user_activity

Functional:
  Split by domain/service
  Ex: orders DB, inventory DB, users DB

Best practice: start simple, partition when
needed. Use consistent hashing for resharding.

Availability = uptime / total_time

99%    → 3.65 days/year downtime
99.9%  → 8.76 hours/year
99.99% → 52.6 minutes/year
99.999%→ 5.26 minutes/year

Achieve with:
• Redundancy: no single point of failure
• Failover: active-passive or active-active
• Health checks: detect and route around failures
• Multi-AZ / Multi-region deployments
• Chaos engineering: test failure modes

Idempotent: same request N times = same result.

Why it matters:
  Network failures → client retries
  Without idempotency → duplicate charges, etc.

Implement:
• Idempotency key: client sends unique ID
• Server checks: already processed? return cached result
• DB: unique constraint on request_id

Retry strategy:
  Exponential backoff + jitter
  Max retries with circuit breaker fallback

Requirements: shorten URL, redirect, analytics

Core:
• Generate short ID (base62 counter or hash)
• Store mapping: shortID → longURL (KV store)
• 301 (permanent) vs 302 (temporary) redirect

Scale:
• Read-heavy → cache hot URLs in Redis
• Counter service for unique IDs (Zookeeper)
• ~100M URLs → fits in memory with hash

Estimate: 7 chars base62 = 3.5 trillion combos

Core components:
• WebSocket servers for real-time messaging
• Chat service: send, receive, store messages
• Presence service: online/offline status
• Notification service: push for offline users

Storage:
• Messages: Cassandra (write-heavy, time-series)
• User data: SQL (relational)
• Message queue: Kafka for async processing

1:1 chat: direct WebSocket
Group chat: fan-out on delivery
Media: upload to S3, send URL in message

Two approaches:

Fan-out on Write (push):
  Post → write to all followers' feeds
  + Fast reads | − Celebrity problem (millions of writes)

Fan-out on Read (pull):
  Read → fetch posts from followed users, merge
  + No wasted writes | − Slow reads

Hybrid: push for normal users, pull for celebrities

Storage: feed cache in Redis (sorted by time)
Ranking: ML model scores relevance, recency

Where: API gateway or middleware

Algorithm: sliding window counter (best tradeoff)

Storage: Redis (INCR + EXPIRE)
  Key: user_id:endpoint:window
  Atomic: Lua script for check + increment

Distributed:
  Centralized Redis cluster
  Race condition → Redis Lua or sorted sets

Response: 429 Too Many Requests
  Headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, Remaining, Retry-After

Rules engine: different limits per tier/endpoint

Types: push, SMS, email

Architecture:
  API → Notification Service → Priority Queue
  → Workers → Provider (APNs, FCM, Twilio, SES)

Key decisions:
• Deduplication: idempotency key per notification
• Rate limiting: per user per channel
• Retry: exponential backoff per provider
• Template service: reusable message templates
• Preference service: user opt-in/out
• Analytics: delivery, open, click tracking

Scale: Kafka for millions of events/sec

Batch (MapReduce, Spark):
  Process large datasets periodically
  High throughput, high latency
  Ex: daily analytics, ML training

Stream (Kafka Streams, Flink, Spark Streaming):
  Process events as they arrive
  Low latency, continuous
  Ex: fraud detection, live dashboards

Lambda Architecture:
  Batch layer + speed layer + serving layer
  Combines both for complete + real-time views

Kappa: stream-only, replay from log (simpler)

Bloom Filter:
  Probabilistic set membership
  "Definitely not in set" or "Probably in set"
  Space-efficient, no false negatives
  Use: avoid expensive DB lookups, spell check

Count-Min Sketch:
  Probabilistic frequency counter
  Approximate count with bounded over-estimate
  Use: top-K, heavy hitters, frequency tracking

Both: fixed memory, no deletions (standard),
accuracy improves with more hash functions/space

OAuth 2.0 Flows:
  Authorization Code: server apps (most secure)
  PKCE: mobile/SPA (no client secret)
  Client Credentials: machine-to-machine

JWT (JSON Web Token):
  Header.Payload.Signature (base64)
  Stateless auth: no DB lookup per request
  Short-lived access token + refresh token

Best practices:
  Store tokens in httpOnly cookies (not localStorage)
  Rotate refresh tokens on use
  Include minimal claims in JWT

Simple: hash map in memory (Redis)

Distributed:
• Partition: consistent hashing
• Replication: N copies across nodes
• Consistency: quorum (W + R > N)
• Conflict: vector clocks or last-write-wins
• Failure: gossip protocol for detection
• Storage: LSM tree (write-optimized)
  Write → memtable → flush to SSTable → compaction

Ex: DynamoDB, Cassandra, Riak

API: get(key), put(key, value), delete(key)

Data structure: Trie with top-K at each node

Flow:
  User types → query prefix → return top suggestions

Optimizations:
• Pre-compute top queries per trie node
• Limit trie depth (e.g., 50 chars)
• Update trie async from query logs
• Shard by first character(s)

Storage: Trie in memory, query logs in Kafka → analytics
Cache: Redis for hot prefixes
Personalization: recent searches + trending

Scale: ~5B queries/day (Google-scale)

Singleton:
  Exactly one instance, global access.
  Use: config, logger, connection pool.
  Pitfalls: hidden global state, hard to test.
  Thread-safety: double-checked locking or eager init.

Builder:
  Construct complex objects step-by-step.
  Use when: many optional params, immutable result.
  Avoids telescoping constructors.
  Example: HTTP request builder, SQL query builder.
  Fluent API: .url().header().body().build()

Factory Method:
  Subclasses decide which concrete class to instantiate.
  Hide construction logic behind a single create() call.
  Ex: ShapeFactory.create("circle")

Abstract Factory:
  Create families of related objects without specifying classes.
  Ex: UIFactory → MacButton+MacWindow, WinButton+WinWindow.

Why: decouple client from concrete types,
swap implementations without changing call sites.
Common in DI containers, plugin systems.

Observer:
  Subject keeps list of observers; notify() on state change.
  In-process, synchronous, tightly coupled to Subject.
  Ex: model → views in MVC, event listeners.

Pub/Sub:
  Producers publish to topics; subscribers consume.
  Mediated by a broker → publishers don't know consumers.
  Asynchronous, distributed, loosely coupled.
  Ex: Kafka, Redis Pub/Sub, SNS.

Observer = pattern. Pub/Sub = architectural style.
Pub/Sub scales across services; Observer is in-process.

Strategy:
  Encapsulate interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface.
  Inject at runtime → swap behavior without subclassing.
  Ex: payment processor (Stripe vs PayPal), sort comparator.

Template Method:
  Skeleton algorithm in a base class with hook methods
  subclasses override.
  Ex: HTTP framework's request lifecycle (preHandle / handle / postHandle).

Rule: prefer composition (Strategy) over inheritance (Template)
for flexibility. Strategy = runtime, Template = compile-time.

Decorator:
  Wrap an object to add behavior dynamically without subclassing.
  Same interface in/out → stack decorators.
  Ex: BufferedReader(FileReader(...)),
      middleware chains (auth → logging → handler).

Adapter:
  Convert one interface into another expected by the client.
  Wrap a legacy/3rd-party API to match your domain.
  Ex: payment SDK adapter exposing a uniform charge() method.

Decorator adds behavior; Adapter changes interface.
Both use composition + delegation.

Stop calling a failing service before it cascades.

Three states:
• CLOSED — calls pass through; track failure rate.
• OPEN — fail fast; reject calls without trying.
• HALF-OPEN — after timeout, try a few; promote/demote.

Triggers:
• Failure rate threshold (e.g., 50% over 1 min)
• Slow call rate (timeouts)

Pair with: retries (exp. backoff + jitter),
bulkheads, fallbacks, timeouts.
Libs: Resilience4j, Hystrix (deprecated), Polly.

Coordinate exclusive access across nodes.

Redis SET NX EX:
  SET lock:resource <token> NX EX 30
  Owner releases via Lua script (compare token).
  Simple but can fail under partitions.

Redlock (multi-node Redis):
  Acquire on majority; tolerates node failures.
  Controversial — see Kleppmann critique.

ZooKeeper / etcd:
  Ephemeral sequential nodes; strong consistency.
  Higher latency but safer for critical sections.

Always: set TTL (avoid deadlock on crash),
use fencing tokens (monotonic ID) to prevent
stale-lock writes.

Speeds up reads by trading write speed + space.

B-Tree (default in SQL):
  Balanced tree, sorted; great for range queries,
  equality, ORDER BY, LIMIT.

Hash index:
  O(1) equality only; no ranges.
  Used in memory engines (Redis, in-memory DBs).

LSM-Tree (Cassandra, RocksDB):
  Write-optimized; memtable + sorted SSTables + compaction.

Tips:
• Composite index: order matters (leftmost-prefix rule)
• Covering index: includes all queried columns → no table lookup
• Don't over-index: every write updates every index
• Use EXPLAIN to verify index usage

How services find each other in a dynamic cluster.

Client-side discovery:
  Client queries registry → picks instance → calls directly.
  + Less hops, smarter LB (e.g., zone-aware).
  − Each language needs a client library.
  Ex: Netflix Eureka + Ribbon.

Server-side discovery:
  Client calls fixed LB address → LB queries registry → routes.
  + Language-agnostic, central control.
  − Extra network hop.
  Ex: AWS ELB, Kubernetes Service.

Registries: Consul, etcd, ZooKeeper, Eureka.
Health checks → unhealthy instances auto-removed.

Run jobs reliably at scale (cron at scale + retries).

Components:
• API: submit job (cron expr or one-shot + payload)
• Job Store: SQL/NoSQL with `next_run_at` indexed
• Scheduler: scans due jobs, enqueues to message queue
• Workers: consume queue, execute, ack on success
• Result/Status store + retry policy

Key concerns:
• Exactly-once execution: leader election + idempotency keys
• At-least-once + idempotent jobs is more practical
• Visibility timeout: job invisible while running, requeue on timeout
• Backoff + max retries → dead-letter queue

Real-world: Airflow, Temporal, Quartz, AWS EventBridge.
